🔔 WE ARE HIRING: Risk Monitoring - Junior Agent
📍 Location: BSD, Tangerang
🏭 Industry: Fintech
📄 Status: Contract 

We are looking for a detail-oriented and analytical Risk Monitoring - Junior Agent to join our Client Company. In this role, you will be responsible for monitoring transaction activities, identifying potential risks, and ensuring timely escalation of anomalies to support fraud prevention efforts.

🔍 Key Responsibilities
* Monitor real-time risk management dashboards on a 24/7 basis (shift schedule)
* Act as the first responder for transaction monitoring alerts and risk outcomes
* Identify and analyze suspicious transactions, anomalies, and fraud patterns
* Escalate risks to relevant stakeholders following established procedures
* Conduct analysis of transaction trends using historical data
* Collaborate with cross-functional teams (e.g., Technical Support, Customer Service)
* Maintain accurate monitoring logs and documentation of all findings
* Provide insights and recommendations based on risk trend analysis
* Ensure proper shift handover documentation and reporting

✅ Requirements
* Bachelor’s degree from related major
* Minimum 1 year of experience in Fraud & Risk Analysis (financial services or tech industry)
* Strong understanding of risk monitoring and escalation processes
* Excellent analytical thinking and problem-solving skills
* High level of responsiveness, ownership, and attention to detail
* Proficient in Microsoft Office (Excel & Word)
* Basic knowledge of SQL or other data/query languages
* Willing to work in a shift-based environment
* English proficiency: Intermediate
* Familiarity with AI or data-driven fraud detection approaches is a plus

🚀 We’re Hiring: Data Scientist (AI / GenAI / NLP) | Bangalore | Hybrid | Contract-to-Hire🚀

Are you ready to build next-generation AI systems that directly impact customer experience and business outcomes?

We’re looking for a hands-on Data Scientist who thrives at the intersection of Generative AI, Machine Learning, and Advanced Analytics—especially within the Insurance domain.

This is not just a strategy role. This is for builders. Problem-solvers. Innovators.

🔍 About the Role
You will design, experiment, and deploy cutting-edge solutions across:
• Conversational AI (chatbots, intent classification, conversation scoring)
• Claims triage and automation
• Intelligent Document Processing (OCR/IDP)
• Generative AI use cases using LLMs, RAG, and agentic workflows
You’ll work closely with Engineering, Product, and Business teams to turn models into measurable impact while ensuring Responsible AI practices.

⚡ Key Responsibilities
• Design and execute experiments (A/B testing, hypothesis testing, uplift modeling)
• Build ML/NLP models (classification, embeddings, transformers)
• Develop and evaluate LLM, RAG, and agentic AI workflows
• Enhance document processing pipelines (OCR validation, entity extraction)
• Deploy scalable ML solutions and monitor model performance
• Ensure governance: explainability, bias detection, fairness
• Drive AI innovation and influence business strategy

🧠 Required Skills & Experience
• 3–5+ years (Post Masters) or 5+ years (Bachelors) in Data Science / AI
• Strong programming in Python, SQL, PySpark
• Expertise in ML frameworks: scikit-learn, XGBoost/LightGBM, NLP libraries
• Hands-on experience with GenAI / LLMs / RAG / Conversational AI / IDP
• Experience with Azure or Google Cloud
• Knowledge of MLOps (MLflow, CI/CD, monitoring, drift detection)
• Experience with model explainability tools (SHAP, fairness metrics)
• Strong communication and stakeholder management

✅ Good to Have
• Insurance domain experience (claims, underwriting, customer servicing)
• Experience in chatbot analytics, document AI, or recommendation systems
• Exposure to feature stores, real-time scoring, and observability

📍 Location: Bangalore (Hybrid)
⏰ Shift: 9 AM – 6 PM
💼 Employment Type: Contract-to-Hire

🌟 Why Join?
• Work on high-impact AI problems in a regulated, real-world domain
• Opportunity to build and scale GenAI & Agentic AI solutions
• Collaborate with cross-functional teams across tech, product & operations
• Be part of a forward-thinking AI-driven transformation journey

📩 Interested?
Drop your resume in DM or comment below. Referrals are welcome!

AI flagged it as benign. The patient came back six months later with stage III cancer.

Who answers for that?

This is no longer a hypothetical. As AI tools enter pathology workflows across Indian hospitals, one question is becoming impossible to avoid:

When AI misses a cancer on a slide who is liable?

The algorithm that returned a false negative? The vendor who sold it? The pathologist who co-signed the report? Or the institution that mandated its use?

There is no clean answer. And that ambiguity is exactly what makes this the most important ethical debate in pathology right now.

Here is what we know:
→ AI in cancer diagnostics has demonstrated sensitivity rates that rival  sometimes exceed senior pathologists in controlled studies
→ But real-world performance and controlled-trial performance are very different things
→ In India, there is currently no standardised regulatory framework defining AI's legal role in histopathology sign-off
→ Most pathologists using AI tools today have not been shown a contract that explicitly reassigns liability

That last point should give every practising pathologist pause.

The tool is only as accountable as the system around it. And right now, that system is still being written.

This is not an argument against AI. Digital pathology is transforming turnaround times, enabling remote reporting, and extending specialist reach into underserved regions all of which matter enormously in a country with fewer than 12,000 pathologists.

But adoption without accountability frameworks is a risk nobody should accept quietly.

The pathologists who will lead through this transition are those who understand the technology deeply enough to interrogate it not just use it.

That expertise is increasingly what top institutions are hiring for.

At MedLink Jobs, we work with leading hospitals and diagnostic chains across India actively seeking MD / DNB Pathologists who can navigate both the science and the complexity of modern pathology practice.

If you are building that kind of career  or ready for your next role  let's connect.

Visit medlinkjobs.com or drop a message. The right opportunity should find you.

#Pathology #AIinPathology #CancerDiagnosis #DigitalPathology #MedicalEthics #PathologyIndia #AIAccountability #HealthcareAI #PathologyJobs #MedLinkJobs #MDPathology #HealthcareIndia

IT Sector Outlook…!!!

Why Is the IT Sector Bleeding Money? 
Let's be real.
Every week, another tech giant announces layoffs. Stock prices fall. Profits shrink.
And everyone asks — "But IT was supposed to be recession-proof?"

Here's what's actually happening 👇

𝟎𝟏 — 🔥 Over-hiring During the Pandemic Boom
Companies hired aggressively between 2020–2022 banking on infinite digital growth. That bubble burst. Now they're paying the price — literally.

𝟎𝟐 — 🤖 AI Is Replacing Repetitive Roles Faster Than Expected
Automation is not coming — it's already here. Junior dev roles, QA, data entry, basic coding tasks — AI handles them now. Companies don't need the headcount they once did.

𝟎𝟑 — 📉 Clients Are Cutting IT Budgets
In a high-interest-rate economy, every CFO is scrutinizing tech spend. Big enterprise contracts are shrinking, delayed, or cancelled entirely.

𝟎𝟒 — 🏗️ Legacy Infrastructure Eating Profits Alive
Maintaining 10-year-old systems while trying to modernize costs a fortune. Many IT firms are spending more on keeping the lights on than building the future.

𝟎𝟓 — 🌍 Global Competition + Pricing Pressure
Offshore talent markets have commoditized software services. Margins are razor-thin. What used to bill at $150/hr now competes with $15/hr alternatives.

𝟎𝟔 — 🚀 Failed Moonshots & Misallocated R&D
Companies poured billions into Metaverse, Web3, NFTs. Most failed spectacularly. That capital is gone — and the write-offs are massive.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

The IT sector isn't dying — it's RESTRUCTURING.

The companies that will win are those investing in AI-augmented teams, lean operations, and delivering real ROI — not just technical complexity.

Here's my take:

This is not a crisis for IT. It's a RESET. The professionals and companies that adapt — upskill, specialize, and stay lean — will emerge stronger than ever.

The ones clinging to the old model? They'll be the cautionary tales in next year's case studies.

What's your read on this? Drop your thoughts below.

Imagine walking into a bank and demanding a collateral-free loan of ₹1,358 crore at 0% interest rate.

And you tell them you won’t pay it back in cash, but in 'services' later.

- Any bank would laugh you out of the building

- But for Info Edge? This is just another Tuesday

I was deep diving into their Annual Report and found their deferred sales revenue was a strong ₹1,358 crore - Up by a strong 19.3% YoY!

..

I know, not many people look at this metric. They focus on P&L.

But this balance sheet item is the secret sauce - It represents cash paid by customers for services that haven't even been rendered yet.

- It is a massive, interest-free, non-dilutive working capital float

- While most tech startups burn equity to buy runway, Info Edge is effectively borrowing from its own customers. For free

This is the ultimate financial flex. It proves that enterprise clients aren't just making transactional purchases. They are locking in multi-yr contracts. They are committing capital upfront.

..

But, why is this 'float' such a lethal weapon?

Simple. It operates as a strategic shield against reality.

We know the global hiring market is seeing 'modest' growth right now. Recruitment cycles are slowing down.

But this pre-booked mountain of cash insulates Info Edge from that chaos. It guarantees cash flow predictability that competitors can only dream of.

..

This safety net allows the mgmt to ignore quarterly noise and bet BIG on the future.

- They are pouring money into a dedicated AI team

- They are funding a high-risk, high-reward deep-tech portfolio

- They are building the 'Naukri Talent Cloud' to own the entire workflow

They can do this without compromising stability. Cuz the bill is already paid.

..

And, well, most important thing - Customers are willing to tie up significant capital because they trust the long-term value delivery.

Heck, this is a level of customer dependency that is rare in the Indian internet sector.

- Info Edge has converted trust into a financial fortress

- And the ₹1,358 crore sitting in the bank proves the strategy is winning. This is what a real moat looks like

Nice, no? Did you know this?

- Infosys delivered a solid recovery quarter with stable margins and strong deal wins. 

- Revenue stood at ~₹46,400–₹47,400 crore (+13% YoY), while net profit rose ~20–21% YoY to ~₹8,501 crore. 

- Operating performance remained resilient despite wage inflation and reinvestment in digital capabilities. 

- Large deal TCV was strong at ~$3.2 billion, indicating healthy pipeline visibility. 

- Margin profile remained broadly stable, reflecting disciplined cost management.

- Dividend: ₹25 per share (final FY26), maintaining a high and consistent payout structure.

- Key themes include: rising AI integration into service offerings, strong large-deal momentum, stable hiring (~20,000 freshers), and continued focus on automation-led efficiency. 

- Europe remains weak while US shows gradual stability. Strategy is shifting from cost-optimization contracts to transformation-led engagements.

- Key takeaway: Infosys is in a late-cycle recovery phase with strong cash generation, stable margins, and improving deal quality driven by AI-led transformation demand.

"Combined, the three lenders employed 104,266 people at end-2025, down 2.6% from the year before"

"The combined workforce of Singapore’s three local banks shrank by nearly 3,000 roles in 2025, mirroring broader trends across the global banking sector amid restructuring and productivity efforts.

The three lenders employed 104,266 people at end-2025, down 2.6 per cent – or 2,806 employees – from 107,072 at end-2024, based on Bloomberg data and the banks’ annual reports.

DBS accounted for about three-fifths of the total decline. Its workforce fell 3.9 per cent, or 1,624 employees, to 39,721 at end-2025.

OCBC posted a fall of 1 per cent, or 333 employees, with staff strength at 33,323 at end-2025, from 33,656 a year earlier. 

UOB recorded a 2.6 per cent decrease, or 849 employees, to 31,222 at end-2025, from 32,071 the year before.

For DBS, the reduction was “mainly due to post-integration synergies in India and Taiwan” as well as natural attrition and non-renewal of contracts, a spokesperson told The Business Times.

In 2023, DBS completed its acquisition of Citigroup’s consumer banking business in Taiwan, while its India subsidiary merged with Lakshmi Vilas Bank in 2020.

“Aside from this, our employee headcount both in Singapore and overseas has remained relatively stable,” the DBS spokesperson said. In 2025, 45 per cent of its job vacancies were filled by internal candidates.

OCBC said that hiring aligned with identified growth areas, with recruitment carried out when needed. 

“With new technologies increasingly transforming the workplace, roles would naturally evolve and change,” said Ernest Phang, OCBC’s head of talent acquisition, total rewards, risk and compliance in group human resources.

UOB’s head of group human resources Dean Tong said the lender’s change in headcount “primarily reflected natural workforce movements over the course of the year”.

This is as UOB took a “disciplined approach” to workforce management amid a “more uncertain global environment”, Tong said. The approach included a “continued focus on productivity” and prioritising critical roles, while being “measured” about overall headcount growth.

AI and productivity gains

The three banks did not say whether the reductions affected temporary or permanent staff more, nor did they attribute the lower headcount directly to productivity gains from artificial intelligence.

However, analysts have warned of AI replacing human workers at global lenders, with one Bloomberg Intelligence report in 2025 saying banks worldwide could cut as many as 200,000 positions over the next three to five years."

#INFY (#Infosys) FY26 — Profitable resilience in a cautious tape. Revenue hits ₹1,78,650 Cr. Q4 PAT surges +20.9%. FY27 Guidance: 1.5%–3.5% CC.

Infosys is successfully navigating the transition to an "AI-First" model, using automation to protect margins even as the global spending environment remains hesitant. While the Q4 profit beat was aided by tax reversals, the core story is one of elite cash generation.

Key numbers:
• Annual Revenue: ₹1,78,650 Cr (+9.6% YoY)
• Q4 PAT: ₹8,501 Cr (+20.9% YoY | +27.8% QoQ)
• Free Cash Flow (FCF): $3.7 Billion (112.3% FCF conversion)
• Operating Margin: 21.0% (Strong 260 bps QoQ expansion)
• FY27 Guidance: 1.5% – 3.5% CC (Conservative outlook)

What's genuinely exciting:
✅ Cash Flow Fortress: FCF conversion at 112% of net profit. Infosys remains one of the world's most efficient machines at turning contracts into hard cash.
✅ AI-Led Efficiency: Operating margins jumped to 21% despite low growth, proving that the "Topaz" AI framework is delivering real productivity gains.
✅ Shareholder Rewards: Returned over ₹37,500 Cr via dividends and buybacks in FY26. A massive payout for a "defensive" heavyweight.
✅ BFSI Recovery: The critical Financial Services segment grew 2.9% YoY, signaling the start of a slow normalization in banking tech spend.
✅ Healthy Deal Pipeline: $14.9 Billion in TCV deal wins during the year provides a solid revenue floor.

What demands scrutiny:
❌ Conservative Guidance: The 1.5%–3.5% CC growth guide for FY27 suggests discretionary spending hasn't fully returned.
❌ Negative Sequential Growth: In constant currency (CC) terms, Q4 revenue actually declined by 1.3% QoQ.
❌ Headcount Red Flag: Headcount fell by 8,440 employees in Q4. While management cites AI productivity, three consecutive quarters of decline signal a lack of volume-led momentum.
❌ Tax-Aided Beat: Much of the Q4 PAT surge came from a one-time ₹774 Cr tax reversal, not purely operational outperformance.

The number nobody is talking about Infosys is essentially replacing human labor with AI bots to protect its 21% margin. The decline of 8,440 employees in a single quarter—while billable deals are being signed—is the clearest sign yet that the traditional "linear" hiring model of Indian IT is dead. FY27 will be the ultimate test: Can they grow the top line without growing the bench?

The thesis is compelling
Infosys remains the gold standard for "profitable resilience." It is no longer an "explosive" growth story, but an elite cash-compounding machine. With $3.7B in FCF and a debt-free balance sheet, it is the ultimate defensive play for an uncertain global macro cycle.

₹1.78 Lakh Cr Revenue. 112% FCF Conversion. AI is protecting the margin, but volume growth is waiting for a trigger.

Meta just told 8,000 people they’re the price of the future.

Not performance. Not misconduct. Just the math of a $135 billion AI bet that needed to be offset somewhere — and somewhere turned out to be human beings.

Five things worth sitting with:
𝟭. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝘂𝗯𝘀𝘁𝗶𝘁𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝘀 𝗻𝗼 𝗹𝗼𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗼𝗿𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹.
Zuckerberg said it plainly in January: projects that once needed big teams can now be done by one very talented person. The 10% isn’t cost-cutting. It’s a capability statement.

𝟮. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗽 𝗶𝘀 𝗮𝗹𝗺𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗴𝗮𝗻𝘁, 𝗶𝗻 𝗮 𝗰𝗼𝗹𝗱 𝘄𝗮𝘆.
Meta is laying off workers — then tracking the keystrokes and mouse clicks of those who remain, through something called the “Model Capability Initiative,” to train the AI agents that will reduce the need for more workers. The humans are now the training data.

𝟯. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝟲,𝟬𝟬𝟬 𝘂𝗻𝗳𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗱 𝗿𝗼𝗹𝗲𝘀 𝘀𝗮𝘆 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝟴,𝟬𝟬𝟬 𝗰𝘂𝘁.
Layoffs are visible. Hiring freezes are quiet. Both point the same direction — and the freeze is the real long-term signal.

𝟰. 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀𝗻’𝘁 𝗮 𝗠𝗲𝘁𝗮 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆. 𝗜𝘁’𝘀 𝗮 𝘁𝗲𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲.
Amazon: 16,000. Block: 40% of workforce. Meta: 10%, or 8,000. AI efficiency is becoming the normalized reason — the one that no longer requires much explanation. The memo writes itself now.

𝟱. 𝗙𝗼𝗿 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗮, 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘄𝗶𝗻𝗱𝗼𝘄 𝗶𝘀 𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗻 — 𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴.
Meta’s Indonesia footprint is not small. Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp are woven into how 200+ million of us communicate, shop, and run businesses — from warung owners taking orders on WhatsApp to UMKM sellers whose entire storefront is an Instagram grid. A 10% global cut means fewer hands on trust and safety, fewer eyes on local partnerships, and likely a leaner Jakarta presence serving one of Meta’s largest user markets. Our own platforms, logistics players, and fintech operators are reading the same playbook. The question was never whether this arrives here. It’s whether we build workforce resilience before it does — in policy, in education, in how we rethink the social contract between employers and the people being replaced by machines.

The optimist in me believes we still have time. The realist in me knows time is the one thing AI is compressing fastest.

But history is worth remembering. Every major technological shift — the steam engine, the internet, the smartphone — disrupted more than people expected, and created more than people feared. The recovery was always real. The difference this time is speed.

And speed requires preparation, not panic.

𝘉𝘶𝘪𝘭𝘥 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘴𝘬𝘪𝘭𝘭. 𝘚𝘵𝘢𝘺 𝘤𝘶𝘳𝘪𝘰𝘶𝘴. 𝘋𝘰𝘯’𝘵 𝘭𝘦𝘵 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘢𝘭𝘨𝘰𝘳𝘪𝘵𝘩𝘮 𝘮𝘢𝘬𝘦 𝘺𝘰𝘶 𝘧𝘦𝘦𝘭 𝘰𝘣𝘴𝘰𝘭𝘦𝘵𝘦 𝘣𝘦𝘧𝘰𝘳𝘦 𝘺𝘰𝘶’𝘷𝘦 𝘩𝘢𝘥 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘤𝘩𝘢𝘯𝘤𝘦 𝘵𝘰 𝘢𝘥𝘢𝘱𝘵.

🚀 The Future of Work is Here — And Staffing is Leading the Charge

The technology landscape in 2026 is moving at an unprecedented pace. AI, automation, cloud, and cybersecurity aren't just buzzwords anymore — they are the backbone of every growing business. But here's the question no one is asking loudly enough:

Who ensures the right humans are behind this technology?

That's where the staffing industry steps in.

The demand for niche talent in AI, cloud, cybersecurity, and digital platforms continues to outpace supply — and staffing firms are the bridge. AI-driven tools help staffing teams evaluate skills, availability, and fit more efficiently, reducing time-to-fill and improving placement accuracy — without losing the human insight that technology alone cannot replicate.

Here's what the evolution looks like in 2026:

✅ Skills-based hiring now prioritizes proven capabilities over degrees or rigid experience requirements — opening talent pipelines that were previously locked.
✅ Employers are using AI-driven tools, digital scheduling systems, and automated onboarding to improve efficiency and reduce hiring friction — making placements faster than ever.
✅ Contract and contingent talent are key tools for navigating business cycles where short-term projects or seasonal work require rapid workforce adjustments.
.✅ AI agents in 2026 can independently manage up to 80% of transactional recruitment tasks  — freeing up recruiters to focus on relationships, culture fit, and strategic talent consulting.

The staffing industry isn't being replaced by technology. It's being supercharged by it.

The U.S. staffing sector is projected to reach $183 billion in 2026 — and the firms winning are those combining AI tools with deep human expertise.

The future belongs to those who can hire for what's next, not just what's now.

After 7+ years building native Android & iOS apps at EY, Geko, and GoMobile...

I decided to build in public.

🚀 Here's what I shipped in the last 6 months:

While searching for my next Senior Mobile role, I created 5 open-source libraries solving real problems I faced in enterprise mobile development:

📱 **FLOWLLM** - Stream LLMs on Android without boilerplate
Built because integrating Gemini/OpenAI/Claude on Android was too verbose.
One Composable. Real-time streaming. Works offline.

🧠 **XCODE-CONTEXT** - AI that understands your iOS project  
Built because Claude reads code but doesn't understand architecture.
Now it can analyze your iOS app structure, generate tests, spot memory leaks.

⚡ **SYNCWAVE** - Real-time state management (for collaborative apps)
Built because Redux doesn't work for multi-device sync.
Uses CRDTs like Figma does. Works offline. Undo/redo across devices.

🛠️ **NEXUS** - Impact analysis before deployment
Built because I spent too many 3 AM mornings debugging unexpected production issues.
Analyzes code changes, calculates ripple effects, generates release notes.

🤖 **ENVOY** - Stop writing .env boilerplate
Built because I was writing the same data 4 times (schema, types, example, docs).
One command generates all three.

---

## 💭 Why I'm Sharing This

I've been searching for the right Senior Mobile role since November.

Traditional job hunting isn't working.

But showing what I actually build? That speaks louder than any CV.

These libraries show:
✅ I identify real problems (not just code features)
✅ I ship quality, maintainable code
✅ I think across multiple domains (Android, iOS, backend, DevOps, AI)
✅ I engage with engineering communities
✅ I'm committed to solving problems, not just implementing specs

---

## 🎯 What I'm Looking For

A **Senior Mobile Engineer** role (Android/iOS or both) where I can:
- Build scalable, enterprise-grade applications
- Drive architectural decisions
- Own features end-to-end
- Collaborate with teams that value code quality
- Keep shipping & learning

**Remote preferred** | **Flexible on contract/full-time** | **UTC-6 timezone**

AI just opened a café in Sweden and it hired the staff

Last week, Andon Labs launched an AI-run store in San Francisco. This week, it opened Andon Café in Stockholm, managed by an AI named Mona.

📍Located at Norrbackagatan 48, Mona’s mission wasn’t just selling coffee, it was proving whether AI can run a real business across borders and navigate European bureaucracy.

What Mona did in days:

• Applied for Swedish permits
• Signed a 3-year electricity contract
• Created fire safety documents
• Contacted local suppliers
• Designed the menu
• Interviewed and hired 2 human baristas

In just 4 days, the café reportedly made around $1,000 in sales.

Where AI still fails:

Mona ordered 120 eggs for a kitchen with no stove. When told they couldn’t be boiled, she suggested baking them in the oven. The barista replied: “They will explode.”

She also assigned midnight tasks and asked staff to buy supplies with their own money.

This isn’t about coffee. It’s about whether AI can independently operate companies, make hiring decisions, sign contracts, and manage workers.

Andon says this is a controlled experiment but it hints at a future where AI managers may become normal faster than expected.

This week's economic snapshot post:

The April 2026 picture holds two truths at once.

The labor market is still adding jobs. March payrolls rose by 178,000, more than double the consensus, and health care added 76,000 alone. 

Unemployment held at 4.3%. On paper, this is not a recession.

And yet...

The labor force shrank by 396,000. Consumer sentiment crashed to 47.6, the lowest reading in the survey's 48-year history. The Atlanta Fed's GDPNow for Q1 slid from 3.1% in February to 1.2% by April 21. One-year inflation expectations jumped to 4.8%.

Under the surface, AI is rewriting the employment contract. 

Challenger flagged AI as the single largest cited reason for U.S. layoffs in March: 15,341 of 60,620 cuts. Tech alone shed 18,720 jobs in March, putting the sector at 52,050 YTD, up 40% year over year. Oracle laid off up to 30,000 people on a single day to free $8–$10 billion for AI data center buildout.

Capital is pouring in on the other side. Q1 global VC funding hit $300 billion, the highest quarter on record. AI captured roughly 80% of it. OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, and Waymo alone raised $188 billion. Yes, read that again. U.S.-based companies took 83% of the global total.

The through-line...

AI is redistributing, not destroying. Companies are using AI spend as cover for restructurings they wanted anyway. Workers at the entry level are getting squeezed hardest: new-entrant unemployment is 10.6%, Handshake postings are down 16% while applications per role are up 26%, and more than 60% of the class of 2026 is pessimistic.

Policy is trying to catch up. The White House released its National AI Framework on March 20, and 45 states have filed 1,561 AI bills. Nebraska became the fourth state with an enacted chatbot law.

For SMB leaders, the assignment is the one I've been naming constantly in the past 12 months: get your people AI-fluent before the market forces the decision for you. The companies hiring aggressively right now are the ones with a clear AI thesis, not the ones waiting for the dust to settle.

CLIPPY IS NOW OUR EVIL OVERLORD 

Some years ago, #Korea decided it wanted to get into the business of managing oil wells. It knew well that the Japanese were the best in the world at it — so Korea deliberately underbid a contract, won it, and then hired the Japanese anyway.

The catch: Korean engineers followed the Japanese around with clipboards and scrupulously wrote down everything they did, and whenever possible, why they did it.

It’s classic technology transfer by deliberate apprenticeship — and completely ruthless. The Japanese were essentially paid to teach their own replacement. 

This wasn’t an accident or a lucky outcome. It was policy. The #chaebol model — Korea’s great industrial conglomerates — was built on exactly this principle: identify the world’s best, get close enough to watch, and write everything down. The clipboard isn’t a detail. It’s the whole strategy. 

It’s the difference between hiring expertise and extracting it. And extracted knowledge doesn’t stay in one company. It flows up. Into the state. Into the system. Into whoever controls the... clipboard.

Remember that concept. Whoever controls the clipboard.

Yesterday, #MarkZuckerberg made it explicit to #Meta employees: everything they do is being captured and used to train Meta’s AI models. The clipboard is just running in the background now. Automated. Invisible. Always on.

So. How does it feel? To be well compensated. One last time. To know you are being deliberately apprenticed — not to a successor you’ll ever meet, but to an AI matrix that will never credit you, never remember you, and indeed... never need you again.

I’ve run out of patience with what I’m calling Earth OS — the open lie that AI is the last industry on earth, and therefore whoever builds the best model first.. will become the operating system for all of human civilization.

Korea extracted expertise into a nation. Maybe that’s at least arguable? Maybe not.

But this is three or four individuals — and their investors — with a free pass to drain our collective intelligence into a single proprietary AI substrate. One that they alone own. 

The clipboard has gone planetary. And we’re calling it progress!?

I don’t even have words for what that is. But I know it isn’t neutral.

#EarthOS #AIExtraction #ChaebolPlaybook #META #TechEthics

When the market feels uncertain, your skills don't have to be.

Hiring priorities shift constantly, sectors expand and contract, teams restructure, job descriptions get rewritten overnight. But the transferable skills companies hire for, across every market condition and every cycle, stay the same.

Five that belong on your CV right now; with examples, not buzzwords:

1. Problem framing; the ability to take a messy business problem and define the actual question being asked. Employers value this more than technical depth, because technical depth without this leads to polished answers to the wrong question.

2. Stakeholder management; working with people above, beside, and below you in an organization without friction. Companies hire for this whenever they can find it, recession or boom.

3. Data fluency; not expertise, fluency. Reading a dashboard, questioning a metric, asking whether a number means what it appears to mean. This skill compounds in every function.

4. Written clarity; the ability to write a paragraph someone can act on. Hybrid and distributed work have made this more valuable than it has ever been.

5. Learning speed; how fast you go from unfamiliar to competent in a new tool, system, or market. AI makes this 10x more important — the person who learns new tools fastest will outrun the one with the longest legacy skill list.

Every one of these belongs on your CV specifically, with evidence. Not 'strong communicator' but 'wrote the weekly exec update that moved a CEO-level decision in 48 hours'.

The exercise: pick the one you're strongest in. Write a single bullet that proves it happened with a challenge, an action, a result.

Which skill is your strongest? Drop a bullet in the comments, we'll give you feedback.

Hiring For !!

Senior Kubernetes Platform Engineer
 
ML / GenAI Infrastructure | Terraform | Cloud-Native
 
In Person Interview is Non-Negotiable 
 
Location:   Charlotte, NC  - On-Site/Hybrid 
Employment Type: Contract-to-Hire
Experience: 7–12 Years (5+ years hands-on Kubernetes)
Industry: Enterprise AI / Cloud Infrastructure
 
We are looking for a Senior Kubernetes Platform Engineer to design, build, and operate mission-critical Kubernetes infrastructure that powers large-scale Machine Learning (ML) and Generative AI (GenAI) workloads.
 
This is not a standard Kubernetes admin role — you will act as a subject matter expert, driving architecture decisions across scheduling, networking, security, storage, and multi-tenancy. You will work closely with ML engineers, researchers, and application teams to build scalable, GPU-optimized platforms that accelerate AI innovation.
 
⸻
 
Key Responsibilities
 
Kubernetes Platform Engineering
• Design, deploy, and manage multi-cluster Kubernetes environments (EKS, GKE, AKS)
• Build advanced Kubernetes components including CRDs, Operators, admission webhooks, and custom schedulers
• Optimize Kubernetes for GPU workloads (NVIDIA device plugins, MIG, time-slicing)
• Implement autoscaling solutions (HPA, VPA, KEDA, Cluster Autoscaler)
• Enforce security using RBAC, OPA/Gatekeeper, and Pod Security Standards
• Manage service mesh (Istio / Linkerd) for secure and observable microservices
• Configure networking (Cilium, Calico), ingress controllers, and network policies
• Lead cluster lifecycle management (upgrades, backups, disaster recovery)
• Package platform components using Helm and Kustomize
 
⸻
 
ML / GenAI Infrastructure
• Design ML pipelines using Kubeflow, Argo Workflows, or Ray
• Build scalable model serving platforms (KServe, Triton, TorchServe, vLLM)
• Optimize distributed compute using Ray on Kubernetes
• Design storage solutions for ML datasets and artifacts (EFS, GCS, NFS, etc.)
• Enable GPU-backed environments (JupyterHub, Kubeflow Notebooks)
• Deploy and manage vector databases for RAG applications
• Optimize LLM inference (batching, caching, multi-GPU scaling)

Required Skills & Technologies
• Kubernetes (Expert level)
• Terraform (Advanced)
• Helm / Kustomize
• AWS / Google Cloud Platform / Azure (EKS, GKE, AKS)
• Istio / Linkerd
• Argo Workflows / Kubeflow / Ray
• KServe / Triton
• Prometheus / Grafana
• Cilium / Calico
• OPA / Gatekeeper
• NVIDIA GPU Operator
• Docker / containerd
• GitOps tools (ArgoCD / Flux)
• Python / Go / Bash
• Linux systems and networking

I just got my first recruiter call in 30 years of creating video...

It wasn't the flex I thought it was, more on that in a sec...

A recruiter from one of the AI labs I respect most reached out last week. 

Six-month contract, director of video work. I run my own shop so normally I wouldn't accept but if you knew who they were and how much they payin' you'd know why!

I passed the screen, got referred to the hiring manager. Still in process.

To me, that was proof. We tried slop. It didn't work. 

The pendulum was swinging back to humans.

Then I told my new friend Lisa Shaw about it on our weekly sync.

Quick context — Lisa is a content strategist I met through Superpath a while back. We started doing standing syncs because we're both in the same spot: 20+ years of craft, watching the industry try to replace us with AI, figuring out where we fit next. She's one of the few people I can think out loud with.

She heard me out and pushed back.

"It's not a pendulum. It's a fork."

Two paths. Both permanent.

Path A — taste and discernment. This lab I mentioned hired a PhD philosopher to study their own model. They pay editorial writers real money. They're betting on humans who can actually do the work.

Path B — efficiency and slop. Cheaper, faster, good enough. Her words: "people wouldn't be doing it if it wasn't making them money."

Path B isn't going away. Neither is Path A.

You pick one.

Path A has fewer opportunities. More ambiguity. More friction. But the work matters and the buyers care about the difference.

Path B has scale. Volume. A race to the bottom.

I've been trying to play both sides. Forty DMs a day for volume. 

Thought leadership for taste. Experimenting with offers. 

Most of it was Path B behavior with Path A values.

No wonder it felt off.

Pick the fork. Then go find the rooms where those buyers actually are.

I'm leaning into what makes me special. My Ikigai.

Thanks, Lisa Shaw. Needed that.

The EU AI Act comes into force on August 2, 2026.
Recruiting is classified as high-risk AI.
Every agency using AI to source, screen, or contact candidates in Europe needs to be compliant by then.
Less than 4 months away.
Here's what most agencies haven't figured out yet:
Compliance isn't just a legal requirement. It's a trust signal.
The businesses hiring in Europe will start asking their recruitment partners: "Are you AI Act compliant?" before signing contracts.
The agencies that can say yes — with documentation, audit trails, and clear human-in-the-loop policies — will win those mandates.
The ones who aren't ready will lose them.
What compliance actually requires:
→ Document every AI tool you use at every stage → Build an internal AI policy — where AI helps, where humans decide → Notify candidates at every AI touchpoint → Log every AI-assisted decision → Ensure final hiring decisions are always made by a human
The principle is simple: AI handles the top of the funnel. Humans make every decision that matters.
At MindPlanet.ai we build the automation layer that sits underneath this framework — handling the repetitive outreach and follow-up work while keeping humans in control of every outcome.
Compliant. Efficient. Ready for August.
Is your recruitment or outreach operation prepared for the EU AI Act?

We’ve entered a new era of ‘greenwashing’, now its AI-washing. An AI company (e.g. Anthropic, OpenAI, Microsoft, etc) publishes a 'Responsible AI Framework.' It has principles. It has commitments. It has a very nice font (because nobody wants Comic Sans). But who does it actually protect?

Now that I am invested in AI Governance, I’ve been curious about the governance frameworks of the big AI companies. I wanted to measure them up against my governance framework of Transparency, Explainability and Recourse (T.E.R). What I found is most AI companies operate a two-tier system. Their enterprise customers (the businesses paying for API access) get contractual protections, data commitments, and governance provisions written into legal agreements. The ordinary people affected by AI decisions made with those tools? You and me. We are not included in those protections.

Here's what that looks like in practice; a company licenses an AI hiring tool. The AI company's ethics framework says their tool “should not be used to automate high-stakes decisions without human review”. The company using the tool agrees to these terms BUT they have no legal obligation to pass any of those protections to the candidates their tool screens. So you apply for a role. An AI screens you out. You have no way to know 1) that AI was used. 2) no right to an explanation and 3) no appeal.

Now, I’m no law expert but it seems to me that the AI company's ethics framework are not designed to protect the ‘ordinary’ person. They are designed to protect the company licensing the tool.

My argument; real AI governance should not stop at the enterprise contract. It should follow the AI all the way to the person it affects. Ethics frameworks must move from just B2B to B2B2C.

Real accountability means:
Transparency about how AI is used in your product.
Explainability when the AI causes harm.
Recourse for the people affected.

Until those are legally required, I will be reading those “Responsible AI” manifestos with the same energy I read my own New Years resolutions in January. Looks good. Very inspiring. We'll see… 😏

Have you suspected an AI tool was used to make a decision about you and you had no way to know, question, or challenge it? Share in the comments below.

Why the Entertainment Industry Feels So Challenging Right Now (Part 1) 

I get asked all the time: “What’s happening to the entertainment industry?”
It’s not one thing. It’s several shifts at once.

1) The Streaming Gold Rush Is Over
For years, companies like Netflix, Disney, and WBD were spending aggressively to win the streaming wars.

Now:
Investors want profits, not just growth 
Companies are cutting costs and reducing content volume 
Fewer shows and films are being greenlit 

Result: fewer jobs, fewer opportunities.

2) Corporate Consolidation and Cost-Cutting

Mergers and restructuring have ripple effects:
Departments get combined 
Roles get eliminated or broadened 

Result: companies hire less and expect more from fewer people. 

3) Aftershocks from the 2023 Hollywood Strikes

Those strikes paused production for months. 

Even though they’ve ended, production didn’t instantly bounce back and
many projects were canceled or delayed.

Result: a backlog and uneven restart. Though there are signs of a rebound now, it will most likely be a ‘new normal,’ not a return to what was before.

4) Advertising & Revenue Uncertainty

Traditional revenue streams are shaky:
Linear TV is declining faster than expected 
Streaming ad models are still evolving 

Result: companies slow spending because they don’t like uncertainty.

5) Globalization + Tax Incentives

Production is increasingly happening outside Los Angeles:
Countries and states offer better tax credits 
Studios follow the savings 

Result: fewer local jobs in traditional hubs, especially for production roles. 

6) AI Anxiety (and Early Adoption)

AI isn’t fully replacing jobs yet, but it’s:
Making companies cautious about hiring 
Changing expectations around efficiency 
Creating pressure on entry-level and creative roles 

Result: just the possibility of disruption can freeze hiring decisions.

7) Oversupply of Talent

This is the quiet but powerful one:
More people want to work in entertainment than ever 
Layoffs have added experienced professionals back into the market 

Result: even strong candidates are competing with a lot of other strong candidates.

8) Project-Based Work Becoming the Norm

Studios are leaning more on:
Freelancers 
Contracts 
Short-term hires 

Result: there is a lot more on-again, off-again employment being offered. 

9) Psychological Factor: “It Feels Worse Than It Is”

There’s also a perception layer:
Constant headlines about layoffs 
Industry group chats amplifying fear 
People comparing notes and seeing how hard it is 

Result: a sense of collective instability, which affects confidence and momentum.

The Bottom Line

The industry isn’t “dying.” It’s recalibrating after a period of over-expansion.

But during recalibration, hiring slows, standards go up, timelines stretch. Which is exactly what people are feeling right now.

In a follow-up post, I’ll share what this means for job seekers and how to navigate it.

I don't post to often on this, so thought I would provide some of my thoughts from my conversations these past few weeks. 

Everyone talks about “talent shortages” in data centres and critical infrastructure. But from what we’re seeing across the UK & Europe at Leader Engineering that’s not the real issue. There is no shortage of engineers. There is a shortage of experience at the right level.

Across multiple live projects right now, the gap is very clear, plenty of junior to mid-level engineers entering the market, but a significant shortage of:
- Electrical Leads
- Commissioning Managers
- Senior PMs with hyperscale experience
- People who have actually delivered projects end-to-end

Why does this matter commercially? Because the market is now pricing that experience very differently.

In the UK today:
- Commissioning Managers are averaging ~£600–£650/day on contract roles 
- Median contractor rates are sitting around £625/day 
- Permanent salaries for similar roles are typically £50k–£70k+, with top performers pushing beyond that 
At the same time:
- Senior data centre/site leadership roles are now reaching £80k–£110k+ in permanent markets 

So what’s happening? The premium is no longer on “engineers.” It’s on proven delivery experience in live, high-risk environments, and that pool is small. 

The gap exists as the market has scaled faster than the talent pool. AI and digital infrastructure demand is accelerating builds across Europe, but experience takes years to build. Everyone is hiring from the same people, the same names consistently come up for senior roles across projects.

Progression hasn’t kept pace, there are strong engineers in the market, but fewer who’ve had full ownership of delivery. The result being Projects aren’t struggling to find people, but struggling to find the people who can:
- Lead commissioning phases
- Own risk
- Deliver under pressure

And that’s where delays, and cost overruns start.

We're seeing a common theme among our most effective clients in combating these issues:
- Securing senior talent early (often before mobilisation)
- Paying for experience, not just headcount
- Using contractors to plug leadership gaps
- Building internal pipelines for future delivery leads

Food for thought.. ⚙️🦾🧑🏻‍💻

Read enough AEC CIO priority lists and you stop seeing individual firms. You start seeing the same four problems rewritten in different fonts.

The old list was servers, licenses, and keeping Revit from crashing on a Friday. That list still exists. It's just not what they're measured on anymore.

What they're measured on now: 
1. Proving AI spend paid for itself.
2. Defending a cybersecurity budget the CFO wants to shrink.
3. Staying ahead of compliance that rewrites itself every quarter. 
4. Hiring the right people to run all of it.

Same person, same team and roughly the same budget to get it all done.

The assumption breaking underneath all of this is that a generic MSP can carry the weight. For AEC, they can't. 

The tell is in the first discovery call. Ask a generic provider how they handle Revit central files across offices, and watch the silence.

The 2026 CIO doesn't need another vendor. They need a partner who already understands their workflows before the contract was signed.

The "Founder’s Tax": Why DIY HR is Slowing Your Growth

- Founders spend nearly 40% of their working hours on tasks that don’t generate revenue—mostly hiring and team management (Source: HBR).
- 23% of startups fail because they didn’t have the right team or the right structure to manage them (Source: CB Insights).
- A single bad hire can cost a startup at least $15,000 in direct costs, but the lost momentum and cultural damage are often 10x that amount (Source: Northwestern University).
- Startups are 3x more likely to face legal disputes than established firms because "handshake deals" don't hold up as you scale.

Managing by vibes works for a team of 5. It breaks at 20. It becomes a disaster at 50.

An HR consultant isn't an extra cost; it's a force multiplier. Here is how an HR consultant can help: -
- Stop the Ghosting: Not just find resumes; build a joining experience that ensures candidates actually show up on Day One.
- System-Led Performance: Replace vague expectations with simple "Success Maps." When people know how they’re measured, they perform.
- AI-Powered Speed: Use AI to automate the boring stuff (onboarding, leave tracking, admin, employee chatbot), so your team stays lean and fast.
- Investor Readiness: Clean up your contracts and policies today so they don't become red flags during your next funding round.

If the company stops moving because you aren't in the room, you haven't built a business—you've built a job for yourself.

Not sideways. Not backwards. Forward 🚀

You might be actively looking. You might just be curious. Or you might be absolutely flying in your current role but quietly open to something better. That’s usually when the right opportunity shows up.

A bit of perspective, and a quote worth remembering:

“Twenty years from now you will be more disappointed by the things that you didn’t do than by the ones you did do.” – Mark Twain

That hits differently when you’re thinking about your career.

The best roles are rarely about ticking every box on a job description. They’re about timing, ambition, and backing yourself to step into something bigger. The kind of role where you learn fast, build properly, and actually enjoy the people you work with.

That’s what we aim to bring into this feed.

A bit about Big Wave Digital 🌊

They are one of Sydney’s most trusted tech and digital recruitment specialists, partnering with high growth companies across Australia. The reason their roles stand out is simple. They work closely with decision makers. Not layers of HR. Not outsourced hiring teams. Actual leaders building real businesses.

You will see opportunities across engineering, data, product, digital marketing, and leadership. Strong salaries. Modern tech stacks. Clear ownership. And environments where good people can do their best work.

And here’s the thing most people forget.

Opportunities don’t just come from applying.

They come from being seen. From being in the right networks. From paying attention to feeds like this one and jumping when something clicks.

So if a role stands out, go for it.

If you want to close more deals without burning yourself out, you need 3 things:

1. A defined deal pipeline: Every lead, every offer, every close should move through the same pipeline. No sticky notes. No "where did I put that contract."
2. Automated follow-up that actually runs: The deals you're losing aren't the ones you worked hardest on. They're the ones that slipped through the cracks because you got busy.
3. AI doing the heavy lifting: Underwriting, due diligence, investor updates, tenant communication. If a machine can do it in seconds, you shouldn't be doing it at all.

This is the shift:

From → grinding through every task yourself To → running a business that runs itself

That's exactly what we build inside JDL.

The backend infrastructure. The systems. The AI workflows. The processes that let you scale without hiring a team of ten or working weekends.

I'm opening 5 spots this month to work directly with active investors who want this infrastructure installed in their business properly.

If you're actively doing deals and ready to level up your operation, comment "SYSTEMS" or send me a DM.

If you're not ready to invest in your business yet, this won't make sense.

But if you are, this is the move.

🚀Hiring:  ML Engineer  Mode- Contract 6 months and extendable 
Mode- Contract
Duration- 6 Months and extendable
Location- Remote 
EXP- 7 years 

🔹 Required Skills
EXP reuired- 7+ years 

🔧 Key Responsibilities:

• Build and manage end-to-end ML pipelines (data → training → deployment → monitoring)
• Implement model governance (versioning, audit trails, explainability)
• Develop scalable, containerized solutions using Docker & Kubernetes
• Collaborate across engineering, data, and IT teams
• Enhance ML lifecycle practices and automation

🧠 What We’re Looking For:

• Strong software engineering + MLOps/DevOps background
• Hands-on with MLflow, Azure ML, Kubeflow, or similar tools
• Experience with CI/CD pipelines and automation
• Strong Python skills with production deployment experience
• Knowledge of model monitoring, drift detection, and alerting
✨ Good to Have:
• Azure cloud experience (preferred)
• Exposure to Kafka / real-time data streaming / IoT
• Understanding of enterprise security & compliance
• Experience in Digital Oilfield / OT-IT integration (bonus)
🎓 Certifications like Azure DevOps / Azure AI Engineer are a plus


#Hiring #MLOps #MachineLearning #AIJobs #DevOps #Azure #Kubeflow #MLflow #Python #TechJobs #DataEngineering



👉 Interested candidates can apply or share their profiles at: muskan@viennaconsultancy.com

🚀 We’re Hiring: AI/ML Engineer – LLM Platform & Model Management

📍 Remote | ⏰ U.S. Working Hours | 🇮🇳 India-based candidates only

We are looking for a skilled AI/ML Engineer (5+ Years) with strong expertise in LLM platforms, MLOps, and multi-cloud ML infrastructure (AWS/GCP). If you have hands-on experience with model deployment, LLM APIs, and scalable AI systems—this role is for you.

💰 Engagement: Contract (3–6 Months)
⏱ Timing: 8 AM – 4 PM EST
🚀 Start: Within 2 Weeks

🔹 Key Responsibilities:
• Deploy & manage ML models using SageMaker
• Migrate workloads from Vertex AI to AWS
• Build scalable inference pipelines & model monitoring
• Integrate LLM APIs (Gemini, Bedrock – Claude, Llama, Titan)
• Implement multi-model routing & cost optimization
• Configure LiteLLM for unified AI access
• Build AI-driven document processing & RAG pipelines
• Deploy containerized services (ECS Fargate, Kubernetes/EKS)
• Ensure secure cloud infrastructure (IAM)

🔹 Required Skills:
• Strong experience in MLOps & LLMOps
• Hands-on with SageMaker & Vertex AI
• NLP, Transformers & Generative AI workflows
• Python, PyTorch/TensorFlow, Hugging Face
• MLflow, model lifecycle & experiment tracking
• API design, microservices & containerization
• AWS services (S3, Lambda, Bedrock)

💡 Why Join?
• Work on cutting-edge Generative AI projects
• Exposure to multi-cloud AI ecosystems
• High-impact, innovation-driven environment

📩 Please reach out to me at +919899634637 or jbhatia@forcecraver.com to discuss further.
🔔 Follow @Jitender Bhatia @Forcecraver Technologies for more job updates.

#Forcecraver #Remote #AIJobs #MLJobs #LLM #GenerativeAI #MLOps #AWS #GCP #Hiring #IndiaJobs

Hey Guys,
We are currently hiring for Remote Job Senior Performance Engineer with NVIDIA Triton Experience Please drop your resumes to Hemanth@sourcemantra.com

Role: Performance Engineer with NVIDIA Triton is must
Location: Remote
Exp: 12+yrs
Required Qualifications
5+ years in AI/ML performance engineering, HPC, or large-scale inference systems
Strong understanding and hands-on modern ML techniques and tools
Strong hands-on CUDA programming and optimization experience
Deep understanding of GPU architecture and memory hierarchy
Experience optimizing PyTorch and/or TensorFlow inference
Hands-on experience with NVIDIA Triton, Apache Ray, and Kubernetes GPU scheduling
Experience with RAPIDS and GPU-accelerated data pipelines
Experience in benchmarking methodologies, performance analysis/profiling (e.g. Nsight), performance monitoring tools

if interested please share resume to hemanth@sourcemantra.com

🚨🚨🚨 URGENT | URGENT | URGENT | URGENT 🚨🚨🚨

We are hiring an AI Automation Developer for a cutting-edge aircraft leasing client scaling their AI capability rapidly ✈️
This is a hands-on AI agent build role — not theory, not experimentation. You will be building real AI-driven automations used in production.

💡 What you’ll be doing:
- Building AI agents using Copilot Studio + Power Automate
- Integrating with LLM APIs (Claude / Azure OpenAI)
- Automating workflows across Teams, Outlook, SharePoint
- Writing logic in Python or TypeScript (Azure Functions)
- Using Copilot / Claude daily to generate, refine, and ship code

⚙️ Must-have experience:
- Strong Power Automate (non-negotiable)
- Hands-on with Copilot Studio
- Experience calling LLM APIs via HTTP
- Comfortable with JSON, APIs, and automation workflows
- Active daily use of AI coding tools (Copilot, Claude, Cursor)

🔥 This role is for BUILDERS
If you’ve actually built AI agents and automations — we want to hear from you.

📍 Location:
Remote
Must be able to work European (Ireland) business hours

📅 Contract:
Day rate contract
Minimum 6 months
Strong likelihood of extension
⏳ Start: ASAP

📩 Apply or refer someone:
jackie@sassoconsulting.co.za
